key_insight: The paper reframes hyperparameter tuning as a statistically controlled selection problem, using learn-then-test to guarantee that chosen settings satisfy reliability constraints with finite-sample error control.
engineering_relevance: This gives practitioners a principled way to pick inference-time parameters, thresholds, and other operational settings with measurable safety or risk guarantees instead of heuristic search alone.
method: Formulate candidate hyperparameters as multiple hypotheses in a learn-then-test (LTT) framework.
Use p-values, e-values, and concentration inequalities to control selection error and certify constraint satisfaction.
Support multiple reliability targets, including average risk, quantile risk, and information-theoretic constraints.
evidence_signal: The abstract emphasizes a unified statistical framework with explicit finite-sample guarantees, but does not report empirical dataset results or benchmark performance.
watch_item: Guarantees depend on properly specified candidate sets and test design, so practical use may require careful calibration and can be more conservative than heuristic tuning.
paper_title: Statistically Valid Hyperparameter Selection: From Tuning to Guarantees
